local_names.romanized_local: Saaf Soup (Hindi romanized); Teḷivāna Cūp (Tamil romanized)

region_of_origin: India (North Indian cuisine); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03301710 represents a generic clear soup food product; used as closest parent as no North Indian-specific clear soup term exists in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers soups and broths as a category; no specific code for North Indian clear soup exists; closest parent used.

main_ingredients: spices (cumin, black pepper, coriander, bay leaf)
optional: chicken or mutton pieces (non-veg variant)
allergens: may contain traces of gluten if thickener added

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of clear soup, approximately 250 g, as typically served as a starter or side dish in a North Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Soup, vegetable broth / clear soup — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for spiced Indian clear soup analogue
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for North Indian restaurant-style clear soup exists in any primary database. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for vegetable broth / clear soup (FDC ID 173170, 'Soup, vegetable broth, ready-to-serve') and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN 2017 data for spiced Indian soups. Non-veg variant estimated by adding lean meat broth contribution per USDA SR Legacy 'Soup, chicken broth, ready-to-serve' (FDC ID 173167). Values represent a midpoint estimate between veg and non-veg variants as the menu item is listed as Veg/Non-Veg. Sodium reflects restaurant-level seasoning typical of North Indian cuisine.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of vegetable soup and broth-based soups — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for clear vegetable/meat broth
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for North Indian clear soup. GI estimated at 30 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable broth and clear soups, which consistently fall in the low GI range (GI 20–40) due to very low carbohydrate content and high water content. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 3.75 g per 250 g serving) / 100 ≈ 1.1. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.guidance: Clear soup served in restaurants typically contains 280 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 700 mg per 250 g bowl), which represents a meaningful sodium load for hypertensive individuals; patients should request low-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 30, clear soup has a negligible glycemic load (GL ≈ 1.1 per bowl) and is generally safe for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when sodium is managed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and low energy density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Clear soup is very low in saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and total fat (0.3 g per 100 g), making it a heart-friendly food choice; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention in patients with heart failure or hypertensive cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Clear Soup (Vegetable / Non-Vegetarian) is a light, transparent broth-based soup served as a starter or side dish in North Indian cuisine, including at restaurants such as Bombay Curry And Kebabs in Singapore. The vegetarian variant is prepared by simmering vegetables such as carrot, celery, onion, and tomato with aromatic spices including cumin, black pepper, coriander, bay leaf, ginger, and garlic, then straining the liquid to produce a clear, golden broth. The non-vegetarian variant adds chicken or mutton pieces during simmering, yielding a slightly richer broth with a subtle meaty depth. Both variants are seasoned with salt and may include a squeeze of lemon juice for brightness. The soup is characterised by its delicate, mildly spiced flavour, thin consistency, and warming quality. Nutritionally, clear soup is very low in energy (approximately 12 kcal per 100 g), low in fat (0.3 g per 100 g), and low in carbohydrates (1.5 g per 100 g), making it one of the most calorie-sparse hot dishes available in a restaurant setting. Its estimated glycemic index of 30 places it firmly in the low-GI category, and its glycemic load per standard bowl is negligible at approximately 1.1. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which can reach 700 mg per 250 g bowl at restaurant seasoning levels, relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Clear soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its minimal carbohydrate content and low glycemic impact. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and the vegetarian variant is suitable for vegetarians. In the context of Indian and Singaporean dining, clear soup is commonly ordered as a warming appetiser before a main course of curry or kebabs.
common_user_queries: How many calories in clear soup?
Is clear soup good for diabetes?
Is clear soup low in sodium?
What is clear soup made of?
Clear soup vs thick soup calories

confidence_reason: No exact entry for North Indian restaurant-style clear soup exists in any approved primary database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central vegetable broth and chicken broth entries, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN 2017 data for Indian spiced soups. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for clear soups and vegetable broths. Confidence is medium rather than low because the food is predominantly water with well-characterised minor nutrient contributions, and the estimation basis is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central vegetable broth or chicken broth source entries used for this estimation. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries used. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries used. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a broth-based soup with no added hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central vegetable broth entry. 5. The food is listed as Veg/Non-Veg on the field survey menu; nutrient values represent a midpoint estimate between the two variants. The non-veg variant will have marginally higher protein (estimated +0.5–1.0 g per 100 g) and slightly higher fat. 6. Sodium value of 280 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-level seasoning for North Indian cuisine and may vary significantly by preparation. 7. GI set to 30 (low) as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for clear soups and vegetable broths; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 8. Dietary flags reflect the vegetarian variant as the conservative default; the non-veg variant is not vegetarian or vegan. 9. SNOMED CT code 226365003 ('Clear soup') verified as a valid SNOMED CT substance concept. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 used as closest parent for clear soup food product.

improvement_actions_taken: Added full multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 for North Indian/Singaporean restaurant context.
Estimated nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy vegetable broth and chicken broth entries, cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN 2017, as no exact database entry exists.
Set GI to 30 (low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; documented estimation basis in gi_source and notes.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes per G3 sentinel values rule.
Documented trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central in notes per zero-macro justification rule.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency: all strings in aliases_by_language present verbatim in flat aliases list.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: exact, closest_parent, none.
Verified GI category threshold: GI 30 ≤ 55 → gi_category = 'low'.
